title: Wet-felted lavender sachet
subtitle: A small flat-felted pouch filled with dried lavender
excerpt: Wet felt two flat panels of merino, join three sides while still damp, and fill with dried lavender to make a fragrant drawer sachet. The project takes about 45 minutes and is a reliable introduction to flat wet-felting for beginners.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting practice. Flat-panel sachets and pouches are a standard beginner project in UK wet-felting guilds.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre prepared into a long continuous band with the fibres roughly parallel. It is the standard starting material for both wet felting and needle felting.
The final stage of wet felting when the panel has firmed up enough to withstand vigorous rubbing and manipulation. The felt shrinks noticeably during fulling as the fibres lock together tightly.

content.text: This project produces a small flat sachet about 10 cm square when finished. The method uses the flat-panel technique: two matching pieces of
are laid out, wetted, and felted together on three sides, leaving the top open to fill with lavender. Any colour of merino works. Pale lavender, grey, and soft green all suit a drawer sachet.

items.name: Strong thread and needle (to close the top)

content.text: Place the felting net on the bamboo mat. Pull thin wisps of merino and lay them in parallel strips across a 15 cm square area, all running in the same direction. Cover the square completely. Add a second layer on top, running at 90 degrees to the first. Add a third layer at 90 degrees to the second. Three layers in alternate directions is the minimum for a stable panel. Build a second identical stack alongside the first for the back panel.

Wet both stacks by drizzling warm water slowly over the net. Press gently to saturate without disturbing the layers. Rub a little olive-oil soap between your palms and spread it lightly over the net surface of both stacks.

Lay the net over the first stack and rub firmly with flat hands in circular motions for 5 minutes. Lift the net and check that the surface fibres are beginning to tangle; you should not be able to lift individual wisps cleanly. Continue rubbing for another 3 to 5 minutes. Repeat on the back panel.

Place the two panels on top of each other, wet and soapy sides facing outward. Press them together firmly. Wrap the bottom edge and both side edges by folding a wisp of merino around each edge and rubbing it into both panels. This joins the three sides; the top stays open. Continue rubbing all over the outer surface for 5 to 8 more minutes.
Once the panels are firmly joined, begin

by rolling the whole sachet up in the bamboo mat and rolling it back and forth firmly for 3 minutes. Unroll, turn the sachet 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat until the sachet measures about 10 cm square when slightly open. Rinse thoroughly in cool water to remove all soap, then press out excess water in a towel.

Leave the sachet to dry fully before filling. When dry, spoon the dried lavender into the open top and stitch the opening closed with a few overhand stitches in a strong thread. The felt is dense enough to hold the lavender inside without it sifting through.

items.problem: The two panels are not bonded and can be separated
items.cause: The side edges were not wrapped with merino bridging fibre, or the rubbing was too brief
items.fix: Add thin wisps of wet merino around all three joined edges and rub firmly for 5 more minutes. The bridge fibre locks the two layers together as it felts into both surfaces.

items.problem: The finished sachet is much smaller than 10 cm
items.cause: Fulling was continued too long or the water temperature was too hot
items.fix: Stop fulling earlier next time. Check the size regularly during rolling and stop when the sachet is still slightly larger than the desired finished size; it shrinks a little more as it dries.
